anchors.9-10: No errors; succinct, precise medical register; appropriate abbreviations used correctly
anchors.7-8: Occasional minor errors; does not impair clinical communication
anchors.3-4: Frequent errors; impairs clarity of presentation

anchors.9-10: All clinical data and interpretations accurate; correct medical terminology throughout
anchors.5-6: Some clinical inaccuracies or terminology errors
anchors.3-4: Multiple clinical errors; interpretation errors present
anchors.1-2: Significant clinical errors compromising patient safety

anchors.9-10: Succinct, structured, prioritized presentation; clear assessment with differential; logical plan
anchors.7-8: Good structure; assessment and plan present; minor gaps
anchors.5-6: Adequate structure but key elements missing or poorly organized
anchors.1-2: Severely disorganized; unable to follow clinical reasoning

anchors.9-10: Confident, well-paced delivery; no notes dependency; fielded questions smoothly
anchors.7-8: Mostly fluent; occasional reference to notes; good question handling
anchors.3-4: Frequent hesitation; poor pacing; difficulty answering questions

anchors.9-10: Exceptional empathy and professionalism; creates psychological safety
anchors.7-8: Good empathy; acknowledges emotion; polite and professional
anchors.5-6: Adequate but mechanical empathy; some emotional cues missed
anchors.3-4: Little empathic response; information delivery prioritized
anchors.1-2: No empathic response; dismissive or unprofessional

checklist.item: patient demographics and one-liner (age, sex, presenting complaint)
relevant positives and negatives from history
clear assessment: primary diagnosis stated first
management plan presented in logical order
presentation under 3 minutes without being asked to shorten
used Teach-back method to verify patient understanding
